message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 3/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation.

## Claim under review
"mlx-audio includes forced-alignment/timestamp capabilities (Qwen3-ForcedAligner for word-level audio alignment with start/end times, Parakeet word-level timestamps), enabling a text-to-audio timing map."

**Source:** https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Qwen3-ForcedAligner provides "Word-level audio alignment" with start/end times. Parakeet offers "word-level timestamps.""

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.

Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.

- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Links: [{"title":"mlx-qwen3-asr · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/mlx-qwen3-asr/"},{"title":"GitHub - moona3k/mlx-qwen3-asr: Qwen3-ASR speech recognition on Apple Silicon via MLX · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/moona3k/mlx-qwen3-asr/"},{"title":"GitHub - Blaizzy/mlx-audio: A text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-text (STT) and speech-to-speech (STS) library built on Apple's MLX framework, providing efficient speech analysis on Apple Silicon. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio"},{"title":"mlx-audio/mlx_audio/stt/models/qwen3_asr/README.md at main · Blaizzy/mlx-audio","url":"https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio/blob/main/mlx_audio/stt/models/qwen3_asr/README.md"},{"title":"mlx-audio · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/mlx-audio/"},{"title":"GitHub - soniqo/speech-swift: AI speech toolkit for Apple Silicon — ASR, TTS, speech-to-speech, VAD, and diarization powered by MLX and CoreML","url":"https://github.com/ivan-digital/qwen3-asr-swift"},{"title":"Blaizzy/mlx-audio: A text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-text (STT) and speech-to-speech (STS) library built on Apple's MLX framework, providing efficient speech analysis on Apple Silicon. - upd.dev","url":"https://upd.dev/Blaizzy/mlx-audio"},{"title":"Qwen/Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B"},{"title":"Canary-1B-v2 & Parakeet-TDT-0.6B-v3: Efficient and High-Performance Models for Multilingual ASR and AST","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.14128"},{"title":"Qwen3-ASR Technical Report","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.21337"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these components:

## MLX-Audio and Qwen3-ForcedAligner

The mlx-audio library integrates Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B for word-level audio alignment in multiple languages including Chinese, English, Japanese, Korean, and others.

## Word-Level Alignment Timestamps

Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B can align text–speech pairs and return word or character level timestamps. The model works by taking speech and a transcript augmented with special tokens [time] that denote word-level or character-level start and end timestamp slots, then directly predicts the corresponding discrete timestamp indices for each slot.

Users can generate word-level forced alignment by loading the model and calling generate() with audio and text parameters, which returns items with start_time and end_time properties.

## Performance Features

The native MLX forced aligner achieves 2.6x faster performance than the PyTorch alternative with O(n log n) LIS-based timestamp correction. Additionally, Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B adopts non-autoregressive (NAR) inference for timestamp prediction.

## Parakeet Connection

A Parakeet architecture model with CTC decoder comprising 600M parameters was used in the timestamp generation pipeline, trained on 128 NVIDIA A100 GPUs using the ASR subset of training data.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Forced Alignment and Word-Level Timing Support

Yes, this repository explicitly mentions forced alignment and word-level audio alignment capabilities.

## Direct Quote

"**Qwen3-ForcedAligner** - Word-level audio alignment" with support for multiple languages including "ZH, EN, JA, KO, + more"

## Additional Reference

The documentation includes a code example demonstrating word-level timing:

"for item in result: print(f"\[{item.start\_time:.2f}s - {item.end\_time:.2f}s\] {item.text}")"

## Models Supporting Word-Level Timestamps

1. **Qwen3-ForcedAligner** (0.6B-8bit) - dedicated forced alignment model
2. **Parakeet** (v3) - supports "word-level timestamps" with start/end times across 25 European languages
3. **VibeVoice-ASR** - outputs structured JSON with timing data

Both Parakeet and VibeVoice provide sentence-level or segment-level timing information accessible through their result objects, enabling precise audio-to-text synchronization for downstream applications.

content.text: The primary source strongly confirms the claim. Let me verify the source repository directly via raw files and confirm the Parakeet word-level timestamp capability to triangulate independently.

input.prompt: Does this model do word-level forced alignment returning start and end times? Quote any text about word-level or character-level timestamps and start/end times. Confirm it works with text and audio input.
Quote exactly any text mentioning Qwen3-ForcedAligner, forced alignment, word-level timestamps, Parakeet timestamps, start_time, end_time. Provide verbatim quotes.

content.content:
# Word-Level Timestamp Features in MLX-Audio

MLX-Audio provides several models capable of generating word-level timestamps:

## Qwen3-ForcedAligner

**Exact quote:** "Qwen3-ForcedAligner | Word-level audio alignment | ZH, EN, JA, KO, + more"

This model specifically handles temporal alignment at the word level:

**Exact quote:** "for seg in result.segments: print(f\"[{seg['start_time']:.1f}-{seg['end_time']:.1f}] Speaker {seg['speaker_id']}: {seg['text']}\")"

Usage example from the documentation:

**Exact quote:** "for item in result: print(f\"[{item.start_time:.2f}s - {item.end_time:.2f}s] {item.text}\")"

## Parakeet STT

Parakeet also outputs granular timing information:

**Exact quote:** "for sentence in result.sentences: print(f\"[{sentence.start:.2f}s - {sentence.end:.2f}s] {sentence.text}\")"

Both models enable precise synchronization between text and audio segments, useful for captioning, karaoke, and accessibility applications.

# Qwen3-ForcedAligner Word-Level Alignment

Yes, this model does support word-level forced alignment with timestamps.

**Supported granularity:** The documentation states it can "align text–speech pairs and return word or character level timestamps."

**Input requirements:** It works with both text and audio inputs, as confirmed by this example:

> "results = model.align(audio=..., text='甚至出现交易几乎停滞的情况。', language='Chinese')"

**Output format:** The model returns start and end times for each unit:

> "print(results[0][0].text, results[0][0].start_time, results[0][0].end_time)"

**Audio duration limit:** The model supports "timestamp prediction for arbitrary units within up to 5 minutes of speech in 11 languages."

content.text: The evidence is now strongly triangulated from multiple independent sources. Let me do one final verification on the Parakeet word-level (not just sentence-level) timestamp claim, since that's the one slightly less confirmed.

input.query: mlx-audio Parakeet word-level timestamps start end time per word

content.content:
Web search results for query: "mlx-audio Parakeet word-level timestamps start end time per word"

Links: [{"title":"mlx-audio · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/mlx-audio/"},{"title":"GitHub - Blaizzy/mlx-audio: A text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-text (STT) and speech-to-speech (STS) library built on Apple's MLX framework, providing efficient speech analysis on Apple Silicon. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio"},{"title":"parakeet-mlx · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/parakeet-mlx/"},{"title":"parakeet-mlx 0.4.0 on PyPI - Libraries.io - security & maintenance data for open source software","url":"https://libraries.io/pypi/parakeet-mlx"},{"title":"GitHub - senstella/parakeet-mlx: An implementation of the Nvidia's Parakeet models for Apple Silicon using MLX. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/senstella/parakeet-mlx"},{"title":"Introduction to Parakeet MLX - Parakeet MLX","url":"https://mintlify.wiki/senstella/parakeet-mlx/introduction"},{"title":"NeurologyAI/neuro-parakeet-mlx · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/NeurologyAI/neuro-parakeet-mlx"},{"title":"MLX Audio","url":"https://blaizzy.github.io/mlx-audio/"},{"title":"System and method for dual mode presentation of content in a target language to improve listening fluency in the target language","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/12190752"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about word-level timestamps in mlx-audio and Parakeet:

## Word-Level Timestamps Access

For mlx-audio's Parakeet model, you can access word-level timestamps by iterating through sentences in the result and accessing the start and end times: `for sentence in result.sentences: print(f"[{sentence.start:.2f}s - {sentence.end:.2f}s] {sentence.text}")`

## Token-Level Timestamps (Per Word)

For even more granular control, you can access individual tokens with their timing information: `for sentence in result.sentences: for token in sentence.tokens: print(f"{token.text} [{token.start:.2f}s - {token.end:.2f}s]")` This gives you the start and end time for each individual word.

## Key Features

MLX-Audio supports streaming and word-level timestamps, making it suitable for subtitle generation, video editing, and accessibility tools. The transcription results include structured timing data that allows you to align audio precisely with text at both the sentence and word level.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
The claim is thoroughly verified across multiple independent sources. Let me assess the refutation checklist:

1. **Supported by quote?** Yes. The mlx-audio README literally states "Qwen3-ForcedAligner | Word-level audio alignment | ZH, EN, JA, KO, + more" and provides code returning `item.start_time` / `item.end_time`. The claim faithfully reflects the source.

2. **Contradicting evidence?** None found. Multiple sources confirm rather than dispute. The underlying Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B model on Hugging Face independently confirms word/character-level timestamps with start/end times for text-speech pairs.

3. **Source quality?** Strong. Primary GitHub repo (Blaizzy/mlx-audio), PyPI, the official Qwen model card on Hugging Face, and parakeet-mlx all corroborate. Not an extraordinary claim — these are documented features.

4. **Outdated?** No. These are 2025-2026 features (Qwen3-ForcedAligner, Parakeet v3). Current and active.

5. **Marketing/cherry-picked?** No. This is technical documentation of API capabilities, not vendor hype. The capability is verifiable through code examples.

One minor nuance: the claim says "Parakeet word-level timestamps." The README's headline Parakeet example shows sentence-level, but the deeper API exposes token-level (per-word) timing (`sentence.tokens` with `token.start`/`token.end`), so the word-level claim holds for Parakeet too.

The claim is well-supported, current, and backed by primary sources. I cannot refute it.

input.evidence:
CLAIM CONFIRMED across multiple independent primary sources; I could not refute it.

1) PRIMARY SOURCE (Blaizzy/mlx-audio README, raw GitHub): Verbatim model table entry "Qwen3-ForcedAligner | Word-level audio alignment | ZH, EN, JA, KO, + more" and code example "for item in result: print(f\"[{item.start_time:.2f}s - {item.end_time:.2f}s] {item.text}\")". This directly supports the supporting quote — it is an accurate paraphrase, NOT an overreach.

2) PARAKEET word-level timing CONFIRMED: README/PyPI show not just sentence-level (result.sentences with .start/.end) but per-word token-level timing: "for token in sentence.tokens: print(f\"{token.text} [{token.start:.2f}s - {token.end:.2f}s]\")". So "Parakeet word-level timestamps" is literally true (token = word). mlx-audio docs explicitly list "word-level timestamps" as a feature for subtitle/karaoke/accessibility use.

3) INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION (Qwen/Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B model card on Hugging Face, separate from mlx-audio): "align text-speech pairs and return word or character level timestamps"; API "results[0][0].start_time, results[0][0].end_time"; supports "timestamp prediction for arbitrary units within up to 5 minutes of speech in 11 languages." The underlying model genuinely does forced alignment with start/end times.

4) NOT OUTDATED: Qwen3-ForcedAligner and Parakeet v3 are 2025-2026 models; mlx-audio is actively maintained. Current for the "as of 2026" research scope.

5) NOT MARKETING: This is documented technical API capability (with runnable code signatures), corroborated by the official Qwen model card and parakeet-mlx, not vendor hype or a cherry-picked benchmark.

CONCLUSION ON RESEARCH QUESTION (angle 2 — timing/alignment): mlx-audio does provide a text-to-audio timing map on Apple Silicon via (a) Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B for forced alignment of existing text+audio into word/char start-end times, and (b) Parakeet ASR token/word-level timestamps. Both run natively in MLX on Apple Silicon, matching the pipeline's Mac/MPS constraint. Claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.

Minor caveat (does not refute): Qwen3-ForcedAligner is a forced-aligner/ASR utility, not a TTS that natively emits durations at synthesis time — it aligns pre-existing audio. But the claim only asserts alignment/timestamp capability enabling a timing map, which is exactly what it provides.

content.text: Claim verified as TRUE (refuted=false). The mlx-audio forced-alignment/timestamp capability is confirmed by the primary GitHub repository, PyPI documentation, and independently by the official Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B Hugging Face model card. Both Qwen3-ForcedAligner (word/char-level start/end times) and Parakeet (token/word-level timestamps via `sentence.tokens`) genuinely enable a text-to-audio timing map on Apple Silicon. The claim is well-supported, current (2025-2026), and backed by primary sources.
